text: The surgeon pushes the uterovesical fold down to expose the round ligament, which is visible in the surgical field. Bilateral uterine artery ligation is performed without the use of vasopressin.

Following bilateral uterine artery ligation, the surgeon makes an incision over the most prominent part of the fibroid, with the choice of incision orientation being flexible based on suturing preference.
The surgeon pulls out the large anterofundal fibroid, approximately eighteen centimeters in size, from the underlying capsule formed by the myometrium without using aqua dissection or vasopressin.

After removing the large anterofundal fibroid, the surgeon identifies another fibroid located at the posterior cervical region. The dissection of the second fibroid begins, with minimal blood loss visible in the pouch of Douglas, despite the absence of suction irrigation, due to the clipped uterine arteries.

The surgeon places two clips over the uterine arteries on both sides, noting that the artery size typically does not exceed four to five millimeters, even with large fibroids. After removing both fibroids, the myoma bed is sutured in multiple layers using quill sutures.

The surgeon performs multi-layer suturing on the posterior fibroid using quill sutures, which feature a special barb arrangement. The suturing is done in four layers to ensure no hematoma formation, crucial for the patient's future conception plans.
The surgical field shows minimal blood loss despite the absence of suction irrigation. The surgeon completes suturing the posterior part of the myoma bed and proceeds to suture the anterior part.

After completing multi-layer suturing of both myoma beds, the surgeon uses a morcellator to extract the myoma from the peritoneal cavity. This step is visually detailed and time-consuming, especially for large fibroids weighing over two kilograms.
